answer:
In *Nouveau traité d'économie* (Vol. I, ch. 11), Dunoyer explicitly traces the etymology and semantic evolution of *industrie*, arguing that it originally encompassed *any* skillful, goal-directed action on things (*"an action exercised in things or on things to arrive at a certain end"*), before being narrowed (especially in French) to mechanical arts or manufacturing.[^1] He insists it *should* be extended to *all useful labors*—productive, value-creating activity (*"creating utilities of some kind"*) free of spoliation—encompassing agriculture, commerce, sciences, arts, moral cultivation (*"adding to the value of things or of men"*), politics, and more.[^2] This breadth is essential to his chapter's thesis: such a society of universal *industrie* (not mere factories) maximizes liberty by developing faculties, perfecting personal habits, and enabling peaceful relations.[^3]

If "industry" were the *only* English equivalent—evoking factories, manufacturing, or commercial production—Dunoyer's core argument would lose its sweep. His claim that liberty arises from paired advances in *industrie* (broad productive skill/effort across *all* human spheres) *and* morality would shrink to a narrower thesis about economic growth (*industry*) plus ethics, obscuring how *industrie* itself includes "industries" of intellect (*sciences*), imagination (*arts*), physical perfection (*medicine*), moral habits (*education/religion*), and even politics (*repressive order as a service*.[^4] This holistic civilizational progress—non-violent value-creation in *every* domain—underpins his rejection of domination; a factory-bound "industry" would imply liberty stems mainly from material wealth, not the full rectification and extension of human action.[^5]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch. 11 ("I. Liberty compatible with the way of life of purely industrious peoples"), [translation](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-06038f).

[^2]: *Ibid.*, [¶¶ following "wealth" (p-828c70) through "social state" (p-f7bdb7)](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-4329f5).

[^3]: *Ibid.*, [§8 close (p-4abf4b)](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-4abf4b).

[^4]: *Ibid.*, intro outline [¶¶ from "After which" (p-d37334) ff.](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-d37334); cf. Vol. I ch. 0 [§2: "from tillage to politics" requires "practical skill... industry"](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-d5b62b).

[^5]: *Ibid.*, Vol. I ch. 1 [§4: liberty grows with "industry, knowledge... good habits" in *all* directions](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-583c6c).
